skill.name: ai-social-media-content
skill.description: Guides creators through AI-assisted social media assets using the inference.sh CLI, including short videos, images, thumbnails, captions, hashtags, voiceovers, and platform-specific formats.
skill.summary: Generate platform-ready social media concepts, prompts, captions, and media workflows with inference.sh.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified networked CLI examples and a pipe-to-shell installer command, but most Ruby backtick, weak crypto, scheduling, and reconnaissance labels are false positives from Markdown text. No prompt injection attempt, credential theft, persistence, or destructive command was found in SKILL.md, but the remote installer pattern and external service calls require a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to run curl against https://cli.inference.sh and pipe the result directly to sh. This is a real supply-chain risk because a changed or compromised remote script would execute immediately, although the adjacent install note describes checksum verification and no malicious intent was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit and executable. Confidence is below very high because the same file provides a verification note and the pattern appears to be an installer convenience, not malware.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Execution Is Core Skill Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands and documents many infsh app run workflows that send prompts, media URLs, and post text to external inference.sh-backed services. This is expected for the skill, but users may expose campaign content, media URLs, or account-linked operations to third-party providers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external commands and networked service calls are visible across the documented workflows. The risk is moderate because the commands are domain-specific examples, not arbitrary shell execution or hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Are Expected Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the inference.sh website, an image asset, the CLI installer, checksum documentation, and a placeholder avatar URL. They indicate external dependencies but no evidence of secret exfiltration or covert callback behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly visible and match the documented service or placeholder media use. No encoded payloads, secret variables, or unknown callback domains were found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified As Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick execution findings correspond to Markdown fenced bash examples, not Ruby runtime code. The examples still run shell commands if copied by a user, but the backtick-specific finding is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the flagged backticks delimit bash code blocks. There is no Ruby file, Ruby interpreter call, or Ruby command substitution context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Labels Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings occur in descriptive social media text such as hashtag and shareable, not in cryptographic code. No use of MD5, SHA-1, or another weak cryptographic algorithm was found at the flagged locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are plain content prompts or metadata, not code. The only checksum wording in nearby documentation references SHA-256, which is not a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: Scheduling And Reconnaissance Labels Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The at-command scheduling flag maps to natural-language prompt text, and the reconnaissance flag maps to a creative prompt about an artist at work. No scheduling command, uname call, host inventory, or system discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are inside quoted media-generation prompts. The surrounding context contains no shell syntax for scheduling jobs or collecting system details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The command curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh executes remote installer content directly. Users should prefer manual download and checksum verification before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is the exact pipe-to-shell pattern and it is present in the quick start. The finding is about supply-chain exposure, not confirmed malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Create Social Media Content With AI
content.value_statement: Social teams need platform-ready ideas, visuals, scripts, and captions without rebuilding each workflow from scratch. This skill provides reusable inference.sh prompts for short videos, images, thumbnails, captions, hashtags, and multi-platform repurposing.
actual_capabilities: Provides platform format guidance for TikTok, Instagram, YouTube Shorts, YouTube thumbnails, and Twitter/X posts.
Shows inference.sh CLI commands for video, image, thumbnail, voiceover, avatar, and caption generation.
Includes templates for viral-style clips, tutorials, product showcases, lifestyle content, and behind-the-scenes posts.
Supports multi-platform repurposing from one concept into vertical video, square visuals, and thumbnail prompts.
Documents batch generation patterns for creating multiple content ideas or media prompts in one workflow.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, login, network access, and access to the referenced model providers.
Generated media quality, cost, speed, and availability depend on external services outside the skill.
Does not verify copyright, trademark, disclosure, platform policy, or advertising compliance.
Posting to social platforms requires separate automation, credentials, and review before publishing.

use_cases.title: Plan Weekly Short-Form Content
use_cases.description: Create a batch of vertical video prompts, captions, and hashtags for a weekly posting calendar.

use_cases.title: Repurpose One Idea Across Platforms
use_cases.description: Turn a campaign concept into TikTok, Instagram, YouTube Shorts, Twitter/X, and thumbnail formats.

use_cases.title: Prototype Creator Assets
use_cases.description: Draft hooks, scripts, voiceover prompts, avatar prompts, and visual concepts before production.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Simple Post Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-social-media-content skill to create one Instagram Reel idea for [topic]. Include the visual prompt, caption, and 10 hashtags.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Short Video Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a complete clip plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to plan a 30-second TikTok video about [topic]. Include hook, scene prompt, voiceover direction, caption, and format settings.

prompt_templates.title: Repurpose A Campaign Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to turn [campaign concept] into TikTok, Instagram carousel, YouTube thumbnail, and Twitter/X visual prompts with platform-specific copy.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Batch Production Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to create a five-day content batch for [brand or creator]. Include topics, media prompts, captions, hashtags, and review notes.

output_examples.input: Create a TikTok concept for a productivity tip.
output_examples.output: A vertical 9:16 video concept with a three-second hook, quick-cut visual prompt, short voiceover outline, caption, and relevant hashtags.

output_examples.input: Repurpose a product launch into social formats.
Generate a week of creator content ideas.
output_examples.output: Five topic ideas with matching media prompts, suggested platform formats, caption angles, and hashtag themes.

best_practices: Review every generated prompt, caption, and media asset before publishing to protect brand voice and compliance.
Use vertical 9:16 first for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts workflows.
Verify installer checksums and service permissions before running inference.sh or platform automation commands.
anti_patterns: Do not publish AI-generated claims, testimonials, or product comparisons without factual review.
Do not paste secrets, private customer data, or unreleased campaign details into external generation services.
Do not run remote installer commands without reviewing the source and verifying checksums.

faq.question: What platforms does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers TikTok, Instagram Reels, Instagram feed, YouTube Shorts, YouTube thumbnails, and Twitter/X visual posts.

faq.question: Does it generate final media files directly?
faq.answer: It provides inference.sh command workflows that call external models for images, videos, voiceovers, avatars, and copy.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.

faq.question: Does it post content automatically?
faq.answer: It includes a Twitter/X automation example, but platform posting requires separate credentials, review, and permission controls.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The quick start includes a remote installer piped to sh. Prefer manual installation and checksum verification.

faq.question: Does it check legal or platform compliance?
faq.answer: No. Users must review copyright, trademarks, disclosures, advertising rules, and platform policies before publishing.
